_work.title: Sefer Yetzirah & Letter-Combination Magic
_work.creator: various
_work.rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
_work.blurb: Printed editions, manuscript pages, and diagrams of the *Sefer Yetzirah* (Book of Formation) and the wider tradition of Hebrew letter-combination magic — the Mantua 1562 editio princeps title page and text, the thirty-two paths of wisdom (ten sefirot + twenty-two letters), the three mothers / seven doubles / twelve simples, alphabet-and-zodiac charts, gematria and number-mysticism plates from Kircher, the Tetragrammaton and its vowel-points, and Renaissance Christian-Kabbalah witnesses such as Reuchlin's *De arte cabalistica*.

entries.title: Title page of the *Sefer Yetzirah* (Mantua, 1562)
entries.creator: Printed by Ya'akov ben Naphtali ha-Kohen Gazzolo (Mantua)
entries.summary:
The Hebrew title page of the *Sefer Yetzirah* (Book of Formation) as printed at Mantua in 1562, one of the foundational early printings of the text with its classic commentaries.

## Iconography
A framed Hebrew title page announcing the *Sefer Yetzirah*, set in the dense vocalized type of a 16th-century Mantua press. The layout presents the short treatise together with the cluster of commentaries (such as those attributed to Saadia Gaon, Abraham ben David, and others) that the Mantua editions gathered around the core text.

## Significance
The *Sefer Yetzirah* teaches that God formed the cosmos through 'thirty-two wondrous paths of wisdom' — the ten [[motif:sefirot|sefirot]] and the twenty-two [[motif:hebrew letters|letters]] of the Hebrew alphabet — divided into three mothers, seven doubles, and twelve simples. The Mantua printings were decisive in fixing the text and its commentary tradition for early-modern readers and the later Christian Kabbalists who drew on it. Gershom Scholem and Tzahi Weiss are standard guides to its textual history.

## For artists & game designers
An authentic 'arcane primer' cover: dense sacred type framed as the opening of a book of world-making.

citations.text: Tzahi Weiss, *Sefer Yeṣira and Its Contexts* (2018).

entries.title: Text page of the *Sepher Yetzirah* (Mantua, 1562)
entries.creator: Printed by Ya'akov ben Naphtali ha-Kohen Gazzolo (Mantua)
entries.summary:
An interior text page of the *Sepher Yetzirah* in the 1562 Mantua printing, showing the treatise's terse mishnaic Hebrew surrounded by commentary.

## Iconography
Vocalized Hebrew set in the column-and-gloss layout typical of the Mantua edition: the brief aphoristic chapters of the *Sefer Yetzirah* on the page, expounded by the marginal and interlinear commentaries that the edition collected. The text rehearses the doctrine of the three mother letters (Aleph, Mem, Shin), the seven doubles, and the twelve simples.

## Significance
The body of the *Sefer Yetzirah* maps the [[motif:hebrew letters|twenty-two letters]] onto the elements, the seven planets, and the twelve signs of the zodiac, making the alphabet the combinatorial machinery of creation. This is the seed of all later Kabbalistic *tzeruf* (letter-permutation) practice and of the Golem legend, in which inscribed letters animate matter. Scholem treated the work as the bridge between late-antique cosmology and medieval Kabbalah.

## For artists & game designers
A page of 'source code' for a world: short combinatorial rules mapping letters to elements, planets, and signs.

entries.title: The Thirty-Two Paths of Wisdom
entries.creator: Aviyud613 (modern diagram after the Sefer Yetzirah)
entries.summary:
A diagram of the 'thirty-two wondrous paths of wisdom' — the ten sefirot together with the twenty-two letters of the Hebrew alphabet — that open the *Sefer Yetzirah*.

## Iconography
The scheme enumerates the thirty-two paths: ten [[motif:sefirot|sefirot]] (the numbered emanations) and the twenty-two connective [[motif:hebrew letters|letters]] that link the nodes of the Tree of Life. Each path is labelled, displaying the combined numerical-and-alphabetic structure by which the *Sefer Yetzirah* says the world was formed.

## Significance
The opening line of the *Sefer Yetzirah* — 'by thirty-two wondrous paths of wisdom God engraved... and created His world' — became the architectural key of later Kabbalah, where the twenty-two letters are mapped onto the twenty-two paths joining the ten sefirot of the Tree of Life. This 10+22 structure underlies the Golden Dawn's tarot-and-path system and modern Western Kabbalah. (This is a modern explanatory diagram, not a historical manuscript.)

## For artists & game designers
A literal node-and-edge graph: ten nodes, twenty-two labelled edges — a ready skill-tree topology with built-in lore.

citations.text: Aryeh Kaplan, *Sefer Yetzirah: The Book of Creation* (1990).

entries.title: Tree of Life with the Twenty-Two Lettered Paths
entries.creator: Eliyak (modern diagram)
entries.summary:
A schematic Tree of Life in which the twenty-two connecting paths are each assigned a Hebrew letter, visualizing the *Sefer Yetzirah*'s pairing of letters with the structure of emanation.

## Iconography
Ten named [[motif:sefirot|sefirot]] are arranged in the three-pillar configuration and joined by twenty-two paths, each path bearing one of the [[motif:hebrew letters|Hebrew letters]]. The diagram renders the *Sefer Yetzirah* claim that letters are the connective tissue of creation as a concrete map of nodes and lettered edges.

## Significance
The assignment of the twenty-two letters to the twenty-two paths of the Tree of Life is the great synthesis of the *Sefer Yetzirah* with the medieval sefirotic Tree; it was systematized by the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn, which keyed each lettered path to a tarot trump, planet, or sign. The diagram is the everyday working tool of modern Kabbalistic magic. (Modern explanatory image.)

## For artists & game designers
The canonical lettered skill-tree: each edge tagged with a glyph that can carry an element, planet, or card.

citations.text: Israel Regardie, *The Golden Dawn* (1937–40), on the lettered paths.

entries.title: Kabbalistic Tree of Life with Sefirot and Letters
entries.creator: RootOfAllLight (modern diagram)
entries.summary:
A clean modern Tree of Life showing the ten sefirot and the twenty-two lettered paths in the three-column layout, illustrating the combined number-and-letter cosmology of the *Sefer Yetzirah*.

## Iconography
The ten [[motif:sefirot|sefirot]] descend through the pillars of mercy, severity, and the reconciling middle; the connecting paths carry the [[motif:hebrew letters|twenty-two letters]]. The whole presents the 10 + 22 = 32 structure of the 'paths of wisdom' as a single articulated system.

## Significance
The three-pillar Tree with lettered paths is the standard reference diagram of Western esoteric Kabbalah, descending through the Renaissance Christian Kabbalists and the *Kabbala Denudata* into the Golden Dawn. It visually fuses the *Sefer Yetzirah*'s letter-doctrine with the sefirotic Tree of the *Zohar* and Luria. (Modern explanatory image.)

## For artists & game designers
A polished, legible version of the lettered Tree — drop-in art for a balance-of-forces magic system with left/right/centre columns.

entries.title: The Hebrew Alphabet (Alefbet)
entries.creator: Immanuel Giel
entries.summary:
A clear chart of the twenty-two letters of the Hebrew alphabet — the raw material that the *Sefer Yetzirah* treats as the building-blocks of creation.

## Iconography
The twenty-two [[motif:hebrew letters|letters]] from Aleph to Tav are set out in order with their forms, including the five final (sofit) variants. It is a plain alphabetic reference rather than an esoteric diagram, but it displays exactly the set of signs the *Sefer Yetzirah* divides into three mothers, seven doubles, and twelve simples.

## Significance
In the *Sefer Yetzirah*, these twenty-two letters are not merely phonetic but cosmogonic: God 'engraved, carved, weighed, permuted, and combined' them to form all that exists. Knowing the letter-set is the precondition for *tzeruf* (permutation) and gematria, the operative techniques of Jewish letter-mysticism. The alphabet is thus the indispensable index for the whole tradition.

## For artists & game designers
The base 'rune set': twenty-two glyphs that a letter-magic system can draw on for spells, names, and constructs.

entries.title: Hebrew Letters with Traditional Pronunciations
entries.creator: Squee3
entries.summary:
A detailed table of the Hebrew letters with their names and traditional pronunciations across Jewish communities — a key to the sounds and names the *Sefer Yetzirah* manipulates.

## Iconography
Each of the [[motif:hebrew letters|letters]] is listed with its name (Aleph, Bet, Gimel...) and its pronunciation in different reading traditions. Because every Hebrew letter is also a number, such tables implicitly carry the numerical values that underlie [[motif:gematria|gematria]].

## Significance
The *Sefer Yetzirah* groups the letters by sound and articulation — the three mothers correspond to a triad of elements, the seven doubles to letters pronounced two ways (hard/soft), the twelve simples to single sounds. A pronunciation table thus encodes the very phonetic distinctions on which the treatise's classification of the alphabet depends. Letter-names and values are also the substrate of *notarikon* and gematria.

## For artists & game designers
A sound-and-value key: pair each glyph with a phoneme and a number to drive a spoken-name or numeric magic system.

citations.text: *Sefer Yetzirah*, on the three mothers, seven doubles, twelve simples.

entries.title: Number-Mysticism Plate (Kircher, '666')
entries.creator: Athanasius Kircher
entries.summary:
An Athanasius Kircher plate engaging the number 666 through letter-number calculation, an example of the gematria/arithmology that parallels the *Sefer Yetzirah*'s numerical mysticism.

## Iconography
The plate works the famous apocalyptic number through the equivalence of letters and numbers, displaying names and words resolved into their numerical sums. It belongs to the family of Kircher's combinatorial and number-mystical diagrams in which alphabets, names, and numbers are tabulated and computed.

## Significance
[[motif:gematria|Gematria]] — assigning numerical values to letters and reading equivalences between words of equal sum — is, with *notarikon* and *temurah*, one of the three classic techniques of Hebrew letter-mysticism rooted in the *Sefer Yetzirah*'s teaching that letters are also numbers. Kircher (1602–1680), in his *Arithmologia* and *Oedipus Aegyptiacus*, transmitted this number-mysticism to learned Christian Europe. (The Hebrew alphabet's number-values, not the Greek isopsephy of '666', are the *Sefer Yetzirah*'s concern, but the technique is the same.)

## For artists & game designers
A worked example of name-to-number magic: any word resolves to a value that can gate spells or unlock secrets.

citations.text: Athanasius Kircher, *Arithmologia sive De abditis numerorum mysteriis* (1665).

entries.title: Plate from Kircher's *Arithmologia* (1665)
entries.creator: Athanasius Kircher
entries.summary:
A plate from Athanasius Kircher's *Arithmologia sive De abditis numerorum mysteriis* (Rome, 1665), his treatise on the hidden mysteries of numbers, including their Kabbalistic and magical uses.

## Iconography
The engraving sets out number-tables, combinatorial schemes, and name-and-number figures of the kind Kircher used to expound the 'secret virtues' of numbers — magic squares, letter-number correspondences, and arithmetical diagrams that fold Hebrew [[motif:gematria|gematria]] into a universal science of number.

## Significance
Kircher's *Arithmologia* is a key early-modern channel by which the Hebrew letter-mysticism behind the *Sefer Yetzirah* — the doctrine that letters are numbers and that creation is combinatorial — entered Christian learning. It sits alongside his *Oedipus Aegyptiacus* in treating Kabbalah as part of a *prisca theologia* uniting Hebrew, Egyptian, and Pythagorean number-wisdom. Daniel Stolzenberg and others survey Kircher's Kabbalistic sources.

## For artists & game designers
A period 'grimoire of numbers': tables and squares that can serve as puzzle-locks or the rule-sheet for a numeric magic system.

citations.text: Daniel Stolzenberg, *Egyptian Oedipus: Athanasius Kircher and the Secrets of Antiquity* (2013).

entries.title: The Tetragrammaton with Masoretic Vowel-Points
entries.creator: AnonMoos (diagram)
entries.date: Modern diagram of a traditional subject
entries.summary:
A diagram of the Tetragrammaton (YHWH) showing the various Masoretic vowel-pointings attached to the four sacred letters — the raw material of the name-permutation mysticism descended from the *Sefer Yetzirah*.

## Iconography
The four letters *Yod–He–Vav–He* of the [[motif:tetragrammaton|Tetragrammaton]] are displayed with the different vowel-points the Masoretes placed on the Name (typically the vowels of *Adonai* or *Elohim*, signalling the substitute word to be read aloud). The figure makes visible how the unpronounceable Name was pointed in the written text.

## Significance
The *Sefer Yetzirah* describes God 'permuting' the letters; later Kabbalists such as Abraham Abulafia built whole contemplative systems on permuting and vocalizing the [[motif:hebrew letters|letters]] of the divine Name (*tzeruf* and *hokhmat ha-tseruf*). The vowel-points are central to that practice, since each pointing yields a different meditative pronunciation. The Name's 42- and 72-letter expansions extend the same logic. Moshe Idel's studies of Abulafia are standard.

## For artists & game designers
A 'configurable true name': one fixed set of consonants whose changing vowel-pattern alters its power — a built-in combination lock.

citations.text: Moshe Idel, *The Mystical Experience in Abraham Abulafia* (1988).

entries.title: Leaf from Reuchlin's *De arte cabalistica* (1517)
entries.creator: Johannes Reuchlin
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A leaf from Johannes Reuchlin's *De arte cabalistica* (On the Art of Kabbalah, 1517), the foundational Christian-Kabbalah treatise that brought Hebrew letter-mysticism into Renaissance humanism.

## Iconography
The page interweaves Latin dialogue with [[motif:hebrew letters|Hebrew letters]] and divine names, in the manner Reuchlin used to expound Kabbalistic doctrine — including his famous derivation of the *Pentagrammaton* (YHSVH) from the [[motif:tetragrammaton|Tetragrammaton]] by inserting the letter *shin*. The leaf shows the textual machinery of name-and-letter speculation set into a printed humanist book.

## Significance
Reuchlin (1455–1522), the great Christian Hebraist, drew on the *Sefer Yetzirah*, the *Sefer Raziel*, and Abulafian letter-mysticism to argue that the manipulation of Hebrew letters and divine names was the highest wisdom. *De arte cabalistica* and his earlier *De verbo mirifico* made the letter-magic of the Jewish tradition respectable for Renaissance scholars and shaped Agrippa and later occult philosophy. Charles Zika and Wilhelm Schmidt-Biggemann survey his project.

## For artists & game designers
A Renaissance 'cross-tradition codex': Latin learning splicing in Hebrew glyphs — a model for hybrid magical writing.
